NOTES: Set the cable at chest height, keep your chest lifted (maintaining an extended thoracic spine), and drive your upper arm toward the center of your chest. Pause briefly at both ends of the movement to eliminate momentum.

Press your back firmly against the pad, keep your chest up (thoracic spine extended), and pull your shoulder blades back. Lower down within your active range and pause at the bottom to eliminate momentum. Drive your upper arm toward the center of your body, contracting fully at the top.Load Distribution:Top: 25%Middle: 75%Bottom: 100%

Lie on your back with the bench supporting you, keep your chest up (thoracic spine extended), and pull your shoulder blades back. Lower to your active range and pause at the bottom to eliminate momentum. Drive your upper arm toward the center of your body and contract fully at the top.

Laying on your back have the bench at a slight incline, keep your chest up (thoracic spine extended), shoulder blades pulled back, lower down to your active range, pause at the bottom to stop momentum (inertia), drive your upper arm towards the centre of your body, contract all the way to the top.

Set the cable so it's in line with the middle of your chest. Keep your chest lifted (thoracic spine extended) and push your upper arm toward the center of your chest. Pause at both the start and end of the movement to prevent any momentum.

Set the cable so it's in line with the middle of your chest. Keep your chest lifted (thoracic spine extended) and push your upper arm toward the center of your chest. Pause at both the start and end of the movement to prevent any momentum.
Keep your chest up (thoracic spine extended), drive your upper arm toward the upper part of your chest, and pause at both ends of the movement to eliminate momentum.

Laying on your back, keep your chest up (thoracic spine extended), shoulder blades pulled back, lower down to your active range, pause at the bottom to stop momentum (inertia), drive your upper arm towards the centre of your body, contract all the way to the top.

Laying on your back, keep your chest up (thoracic spine extended), shoulder blades pulled back, lower down to your active range, pause at the bottom to stop momentum (inertia), drive your upper arm towards the centre of your body, contract all the way to the top.

Laying on your back have the bench at a slight incline, keep your chest up (thoracic spine extended), shoulder blades pulled back, lower down to your active range, pause at the bottom to stop momentum (inertia), drive your upper arm towards the centre of your body, contract all the way to the top.

Select a shoulder-width grip and lie on your back. Keep your chest up (thoracic spine extended) and pull your shoulder blades back. Lower to your active range, pausing at the bottom to eliminate momentum. Drive your upper arm toward the center of your body and contract fully at the top.

Pick a neutral grip that feels natural and allows your arms to stay close to your sides. While lying on your back, keep your chest lifted (with your thoracic spine extended) and squeeze your shoulder blades together. Lower the weight until you reach your active range of motion, pause at the bottom to prevent any momentum, then push your upper arm toward the center of your body, contracting fully as you reach the top.

Laying on your back, keep your chest up (thoracic spine extended), shoulder blades pulled back, lower down to your active range, pause at the to stop momentum (inertia), drive your upper arm towards the centre of your body, contract you chest hard and drive up to the top.

Set the cable so it's in line with the middle of your chest. Keep your chest lifted (thoracic spine extended) and push your upper arm toward the center of your chest. Pause at both the start and end of the movement to prevent any momentum.

Set the cable up to line up with the upper (clavicular) part of your chest, keep your chest up (thoracic spine extended), drive your upper arm towards the upper part of your chest, pause at the both end of the range of movement, to stop momentum (inertia),

Set the cable up to line up with the lower part (costal) of your chest, keep your chest up (thoracic spine extended), drive your upper arm towards the upper part of your chest, pause at the both end of the range of movement, to stop momentum (inertia).

Band around the back, hands at a comfortable with, lower yourself towards the ground, pause at the bottom, feel as though you are driving your upper arm towards the middle, this will create an internal force that will help bias the pecs.
Get stable, keep your chest up (thoracic spine extended), and pull your elbows back. Lower yourself within your active range of motion, pause at the bottom, then push yourself back up to the top.

`:
Banded Tricep Dip
(Band around your feet)
NOTES: Place the band around the bottom and either hook your feet or knees in it. Get stable, keep your chest up (thoracic spine extended), and pull your elbows back. Lower yourself within your active range of motion, pause at the bottom, then push yourself back up to the top.
Keep your thoracic spine extended and brace your abs tightly. Maintain external rotation and push upward, focusing on driving your upper arm toward your head.

Set the bench at an angle that allows you to stay within your active range of external rotation. Focus on driving your upper arm toward your ear. Keep your scapula retracted the whole time and brace your abs as tightly as possible. Pause at the bottom of the rep to eliminate any momentum.

Adjust the bench to the specified angle and select a grip that feels comfortable. Focus on driving your upper arm toward your ear. Keep your scapula retracted throughout and brace your abs tightly. Pause at the bottom of the rep to prevent any momentum.

Brace your abs and focus on driving your upper arm down. You can perform this movement with one arm at a time (unilaterally) or both arms together (bilaterally). Contract fully at the top of the movement.

Lie on your back and set the cable so it lines up with your delt. Adduct your upper arm away from your body. If you're using cuffs, they’ll help eliminate the need to grip. Contract fully at the top of the movement.

Lie chest-down on a bench set at a 45-60 degree angle. Brace your abs, keeping your spine neutral or slightly extended (chest up). Drive your upper arm toward the base of your spine, contracting your back as hard as possible. Control the eccentric (lowering) part of the movement.
Set the bench at an 80-90 degree angle. Focus on driving your upper arm outward like an "X," emphasizing external rotation of the upper arm. Contract hard at the top of the movement.
Brace your abs and focus on driving your upper arm outward, not backward. Make sure your arms don't drop below shoulder level, as that will reduce the tension.
Whether unilateral or bilateral, set the cable so it runs through your medial delt. Position the cable at a height where the load will decrease at the top of the movement.

Brace your abs and keep your arms by your sides. Adduct your upper arm away from your body and slightly in front of you. During the eccentric phase, avoid lowering your arm directly beneath you, as this will reduce the tension on the delt.

Brace your abs and keep your arms by your sides. Adduct your upper arm in a ‘Y’ shape in front of you. During the eccentric phase, avoid lowering your arm directly beneath you, as this will reduce tension on the delt.
Brace your abs and drive your elbows outward. Position the cable so it runs through your rear delts, focusing on moving outward rather than backward.
Brace your abs and drive your elbows outward. Set the cable to run through your rear delts, with the opposite cable in your other hand. Focus on moving outward, not backward.
Brace your abs and focus on driving your upper arm outward, not backward. Make sure your arms don't drop below shoulder level, as that will reduce the tension.
Set the cable slightly above your head and drive your arms apart. Focus on pushing your arms outward and contracting your rear delts as hard as possible.

Laying on your back have the bench at a slight incline, keep your chest up (thoracic spine extended), shoulder blades pulled back, lower down to your active range, pause at the bottom to stop momentum (inertia), drive your upper arm towards the centre of your body, contract all the way to the top.
Set the cable to the lowest position and brace your abs tightly. Flex from the elbow, ensuring your bicep is in a fully stretched position at the bottom of the rep.
Sit upright and brace your abs tightly. Flex your elbow, contracting your bicep all the way to the top.
Position yourself so your upper arm is perpendicular to the floor. Press your upper arm into the pad and contract your bicep fully as you reach the top.
Press your upper arm into the pad, keeping it aligned straight up and down. Contract your bicep as hard as possible while continuing to drive your arm into the pad.

Use a neutral grip and brace your abs tightly. Minimize any shoulder movement and focus on flexing from the elbow. Contract your biceps as hard as you can. Lean to the side you're curling to align your upper arm with the dumbbell, which will be better for joint health.
Set the cable above shoulder height and aim to align it with your bicep. If possible, brace your upper arm against a pad to help with stability.
Set the cable to the lowest setting and attach the rope. Flex at the elbow joint, contracting your biceps fully as you reach the top.

Brace your abs tightly and minimize any shoulder movement. Focus on flexing from the elbow joint and contract your biceps as hard as possible. Lean toward the side you're curling to align your upper arm with the dumbbell, which will be better for joint health.

Align the cable with the arm you want to work and set it to the lowest setting. Brace your abs tightly, flex at the elbow, and make sure your bicep is in a fully stretched position at the bottom of the rep.

`: Dual Arm Cable Cross Body Tricep Extension
NOTES: Set the cable at the top of the stack and grab either the ball on the end of the cable or a cuff. Hold the left cable in your right hand and the right cable in your left hand. Pull your shoulder blades back and down, then extend from the elbow joint.
Training Video: Dual Arm Cross Body Tricep Extension

NOTES: Set the cable at the top of the stack and grab the rope (preferably a long rope, or combine two ropes). Pull your shoulder blades back and down, then extend from the elbow.

Set up the cable so you can perform an extension from behind the head. Ensure your arm has a smooth path and your shoulder is in a comfortable position. This setup will target the tricep in its lengthened range.

`: Single Arm Cross Body Cable Tricep Extension
NOTES: Set the cable at the top of the stack and grab either the ball at the end of the cable or a cuff. Pull your shoulder blades back and down, then extend from the elbow joint.
Training Video: Single Arm Cross Body Tricep Extension

NOTES: Pick up both dumbbells and lie on your back. Keep your elbows above your shoulders, extend from the elbow joint, and contract your triceps as hard as possible. Lower the weights down to your shoulder joint.
Adjust the seat to better align with the machine. Drive your tricep into the pad, extend from the elbow, and contract your tricep as hard as possible.

Choose a shoulder-width grip and lie on your back. Keep your chest lifted (thoracic spine extended) and retract your shoulder blades. Lower the weight within your active range, pausing at the bottom to stop momentum. Drive your upper arm toward the center of your body and contract fully at the top.

Choose a foot position that feels comfortable and brace your abs tightly. Keep your spine neutral, flex from the knee, and control the descent to the bottom. Pause at the bottom, then drive all the way up to the top.

Choose a foot position that feels comfortable and brace your abs tightly. Keep your spine neutral, flex from the knee, and control the descent to the bottom. Pause at the bottom, then drive all the way up to the top.

Choose a foot position that feels comfortable and brace your abs tightly. Keep your spine neutral, flex from the knee, and control the descent to the bottom. Pause at the bottom, then drive all the way up to the top.

Choose a foot position that feels comfortable and brace your abs tightly. Keep your spine neutral, flex from the knee, and control the descent to the bottom. Pause at the bottom, then drive all the way up to the top.
Brace your abs and push your hips back with slightly bent knees. Continue driving your hips back until you reach your active range.

Grab the bar and brace your abs tightly. Pull your lats down and back while keeping your spine neutral. Flex at the knee joint, aiming to bring your hamstrings close to your calves. Pause at the bottom, keep your knees pushed out, and contract all the way to the top.

Grab onto the bar, brace your abs as hard as you can, pull your lats down and back, keep you spine neutral, flex from the knee joint, try and make your hamstrings touch your calves, pause at the bottom, keep driving your knees out, contract all the way up to the top

Grab onto the safety bar and brace your abs tightly. Pull your lats down and back while maintaining a neutral spine. Flex at the knee joint, aiming to bring your hamstrings close to your calves. Pause at the bottom, keep driving your knees out, and contract fully as you return to the top.

Grab the bar in a front rack position and brace your abs tightly. Pull your lats down and back, maintaining a neutral spine. Flex at the knee joint, aiming to bring your hamstrings close to your calves. Pause at the bottom, continue driving your knees out, and contract fully as you return to the top.

Use either a sissy squat machine or a barbell for support. If you're using the sissy squat machine, perform a squat, then push your hips back to keep your quads under tension. If you're using a barbell, rise onto your toes, drive your knees forward, pause at the bottom, and then push back up to the top.
Keep your chest up, drive your front leg forward, and push your knee out to the side. Lower your back leg to the bottom, then drive through your front leg to return to the top and swap sides.

For a single-leg exercise, choose a position that feels comfortable. Brace your abs tightly and keep your spine neutral. Flex from the knee, controlling the movement as you lower to the bottom. Pause at the bottom, then drive all the way up to the top.

In a split lunge position with your front foot elevated, drive your front knee as far forward as possible. Your back leg should be in hip extension, and most of your weight should be on the leading leg. Pause at the bottom, then drive up to the top.

`:
Heel Wedged DB Split Squat 
(Rear Foot Eleveated)
NOTES: In a split lunge position with your rear foot elevated, drive your front knee as far forward as you can. Your back leg should be in hip extension, and most of your weight should be on the leading leg. Pause at the bottom, then drive up to the top.
Training Video: Heel Wedged Rear Foot Elevated Split Squat

NOTES: In a split lunge position with the safety bar, drive your front knee as far forward as possible while keeping the back leg in hip extension. Most of your weight should be on the leading leg. Pause at the bottom of the rep, then drive up to the top.
Align your knee with the axis of the machine and grip the handles tightly. Drive your hamstrings into the pad, extend from the knee joint, and contract fully at the top.
Sit upright and make sure you're pulling yourself into the machine. Pause at both the start and end of the range of motion (ROM).
Sit upright and make sure you're pulling yourself into the machine. Pause at both the start and end of the range of motion (ROM).

Pick a foot position that feels comfortable, brace your abs tightly, and keep your spine neutral. Flex at the knee, controlling the movement as you lower yourself to the bottom. Pause at the bottom, then drive all the way up to the top.

Pick a foot position that feels comfortable, brace your abs tightly, and keep your spine neutral. Flex at the knee, controlling the movement as you lower yourself to the bottom. Pause at the bottom, then drive all the way up to the top.

Choose a comfortable foot position, brace your abs tightly, and maintain a neutral spine. Flex your knee and lower yourself slowly to the bottom, controlling the movement. Pause at the bottom, then push all the way back up to the top.
Brace your abs, press your upper legs firmly against the pads, and maintain a neutral spine. Control the movement as you rise, contracting your glutes and hamstrings hard at the top.

Brace your abs tightly, retract your scapula, and externally rotate your femur. Remove the slack from the bar, then drive up. For the eccentric phase, maintain tension, drive your hips back within your active range (without moving your lumbar spine), pause at the bottom, and then drive back up to the top.

Choose a foot position that feels comfortable, brace your abs tightly, and keep your spine neutral. Flex at the knee, controlling the movement as you lower yourself to the bottom. Pause at the bottom, then drive all the way up to the top.

Lying prone on the machine, keep your hips down against the pad, brace your abs as hard as you can. Flex from the knee joint, contracting your hamstrings all the way at the top. Lower the weight down, stopping just shy of fully extending to maintain tension.
Kneeling or standing, drive the leg you're using into the pad. Contract your hamstrings as hard as you can, keeping your abs braced throughout the movement to limit any lumbar movement.
Kneeling or standing, drive the leg you're using into the pad. Contract your hamstrings as hard as you can, keeping your abs braced throughout the movement to limit any lumbar movement.
Abs braced, choose a foot placement that feels comfortable. Keep your abs contracted throughout the movement, drive your knees out, and contract your glutes all the way to the top.

Abs braced, with a barbell or dumbbell on your hips, drive your hips towards the top. Think about driving your knees out and keep your abs contracted throughout. Focus on contracting your glutes hard as you reach the top of the movement.

`:
Dual Cable D-Handle Lat Pulldown 
(Sitting prone against high inclined bench)
NOTES: For a lat-biased movement, use a bilateral grip, brace your abs, and extend your thoracic spine. Drive your elbows down toward the base of your spine, focusing on depressing the scapula. On the eccentric phase, drive your elbows forward and maintain tension in your lats. For an upper-back bias, maintain the same bilateral grip, but focus on driving your elbows wide. A pronated grip will assist with this. Concentrate on retracting your scapula to better engage the upper back.

Place your hands in a prone position or grip the D-handles. Keep your core tight, pull your elbow toward the base of your spine, and squeeze at the top. Slowly lower yourself back down, pause at the bottom, and repeat.
Use either a barbell or D-handles. Sit in an L-sit position on a seat or stacked plates about hip height. Push your chest toward the bar and focus on driving your upper arms down toward your hips.

Start with the bar on the floor, and make sure it returns to the floor after each rep to eliminate momentum. Use a prone grip and keep your core tight. Hinge at the hips until your torso is bent at a 45–60-degree angle. Pull your upper arms toward the base of your spine, squeezing your lats and traps hard at the top. Control the lowering phase.

Lie face down on a bench set to a 45–60-degree angle. Keep your core tight and your spine neutral or slightly extended with your chest up. Pull your upper arms toward the base of your spine, squeezing your back muscles hard at the top. Control the lowering phase of the movement.

With your chest supported on a flat bench, brace your core and keep your spine neutral or slightly extended (chest up). Pull your upper arms toward the base of your spine, squeezing your back muscles tightly. Control the lowering phase of the movement.

Use a T-bar handle with a prone grip. Brace your core and hinge at the hips until your torso is at a 45–60-degree angle. Pull your upper arms toward the base of your spine, squeezing your lats and traps hard at the top. Control the lowering phase.

Set the bar 2–3 inches below your knees. Brace your core tightly, retract your shoulder blades, and externally rotate your femurs. Take the slack out of the bar, then drive up. For the eccentric, keep everything engaged and push your hips back. You can either touch and go or reset with a dead stop between reps.

Position the bar 2–3 inches below your knees. Brace your core, retract your shoulder blades, and externally rotate your femurs. Take the slack out of the bar, then drive it up. During the eccentric, stay tight and push your hips back. You can either touch and go or reset with a dead stop between reps.

Use a prone grip and brace your core. Hinge at the hips until your torso is bent at a 45–60-degree angle. Pull your upper arms toward the base of your spine, squeezing your lats and traps hard at the top. Control the lowering phase.

Pick a grip that feels comfortable for you, brace your core, and hinge at the hips until your torso is at a 45–60-degree angle. Pull your upper arms toward the base of your spine, squeezing your lats and traps at the top. Control the lowering phase.

Tighten your core, retract your shoulder blades, and externally rotate your femurs. Take the slack out of the bar, then drive up. As you start the eccentric, keep everything engaged and push your hips back. You can either touch and go or reset with a dead stop.

Brace your core and extend your thoracic spine. For a more lat-focused movement, drive your elbow down toward the base of your spine, concentrating on scapular depression. During the eccentric, bring your elbow forward while maintaining tension in your lats. For an upper back focus, think about driving your elbows wide. A pronated grip will help, and focus on retracting your scapula while keeping your chest pressed into the pad.

Set up the cable so it's aligned with both of your lats, as best as you can. Focus on driving your upper arm toward the base of your spine, which will help target and engage your lats more effectively.

`:
Chest Supported Machine Row
(Single Arm or Dual)
NOTES: Set up the cable so it's aligned with both of your lats, as best as you can. Focus on driving your upper arm toward the base of your spine, which will help target and engage your lats more effectively.
Training Video: Chest Supported Machine Row (Single Arm or Dual)

`: Seated Chest Supported D-Handle Cable Row
NOTES: Brace your core and extend your thoracic spine. For a more lat-focused movement, drive your elbow down toward the base of your spine, focusing on scapular depression. On the eccentric, bring your elbow forward while keeping tension on your lats. To target the upper back more, think about driving your elbows wide. A pronated grip will assist, and focus on retracting your scapula.

Brace your core and extend your thoracic spine. For a more lat-focused movement, drive your elbow down toward the base of your spine, concentrating on scapular depression. During the eccentric, bring your elbow forward while maintaining tension on your lats. To target the upper back more, think about driving your elbows wide. A pronated grip will help, and focus on retracting your scapula.

Make sure your leading leg is the same side as the arm you're rowing with. Hinge at the hips, brace your core, and drive your elbow toward the base of your spine. During the eccentric, bring your elbow down toward the floor.

`: Single Arm Chest Supported D-Handle Cable Pulldown
NOTES: With a bilateral setup, brace your core and extend your thoracic spine. For a more lat-focused movement, drive your elbow down toward the base of your spine, focusing on depressing your scapula. During the eccentric, bring your elbow forward while keeping tension in your lats. For more upper back engagement, think about driving your elbows wide. A pronated grip will assist, and focus on retracting your scapula.
Training Video: Single Arm Chest Supported D-Handle Cable Pulldown

NOTES: Whether unilateral or bilateral, brace your core and extend your thoracic spine. For a more lat-focused movement, drive your elbow down toward the base of your spine, concentrating on scapular depression. During the eccentric, bring your elbow forward while keeping tension on your lats. For more upper back engagement, think about driving your elbows wide. A pronated grip will help, focus on retracting your scapula, and keep your chest pressed into the pad.

Whether unilateral or bilateral, brace your core and extend your thoracic spine. Drive your elbow down toward the base of your spine, and during the eccentric, bring your elbow forward while maintaining tension in your lat.

Whether unilateral or bilateral, brace your core and extend your thoracic spine. Drive your elbow down toward the base of your spine, and during the eccentric, bring your elbow forward while maintaining tension in your lat.
Training Video: Chest Supported Machine Single Arm Pulldown

`: Panata Bent Over Row Machine (Chest Supported)
NOTES: Whether unilateral or bilateral, brace your core and extend your thoracic spine. For a more lat-focused movement, drive your elbow down to the base of your spine, focusing on scapular depression. On the eccentric, bring your elbow forward while maintaining tension in your lats. For more upper back activation, think about driving your elbows wide. A pronated grip will assist, focus on retracting your scapula, and keep your chest pressed into the pad.
Training Video: Panata Bent Over Row Machine (chest Supported)

NOTES: Whether unilateral or bilateral, brace your core and extend your thoracic spine. For a more lat-focused movement, drive your elbow down to the base of your spine, focusing on scapular depression. On the eccentric, bring your elbow forward while maintaining tension in your lats. For more upper back activation, think about driving your elbows wide. A pronated grip will assist, focus on retracting your scapula, and keep your chest pressed into the pad.

Brace your core tightly, retract your shoulder blades, and externally rotate your femurs. Take the slack out of the bar, then drive up. As you begin the eccentric phase, keep everything engaged, push your hips back, and stay within your active range (the point where you can push your hips back without moving your lower back). Pause, then drive back up to the top.

Place your hands in a prone position or use D-handles, keeping your core tight. Pull your elbow toward the base of your spine, contracting fully at the top. Lower yourself back down, pause at the bottom, and repeat.

With a bilateral setup, brace your core and extend your thoracic spine. For a lat-focused movement, drive your elbow down to the base of your spine, emphasizing scapular depression. On the eccentric phase, bring your elbow forward while maintaining tension in your lats. For an upper back focus, think about driving your elbows wide. A pronated grip will assist, and focus on retracting your scapula.
For a unilateral setup, position the cable so it's aligned with the lat you're targeting. Drive your upper arm toward the base of your spine to better engage and bias your lat.

Stand with your weight on the balls of your feet, and drive up onto your big toe. Contract your calf as hard as you can all the way to the top, then lower back down to the bottom position, maintaining tension in your calf throughout the movement.

Sit with your feet on the base of your toes, then drive up onto your big toe. Contract your calf as hard as you can all the way to the top, then slowly lower down to the bottom position, keeping tension in your calf throughout the entire movement.

Sit with your feet on the base of your toes, then drive up onto your big toe. Contract your calf as hard as you can all the way to the top, then slowly lower down to the bottom position, keeping tension in your calf throughout the entire movement.

Push as hard as you can to concentrically load the lower body. This will pump a lot of blood into the muscle without the usual eccentric damage from heavy lifting. Plus, there’s a cardiovascular benefit to this type of movement.

Push as hard as you can to concentrically load the lower body. This will pump a lot of blood into the muscle without the usual eccentric damage from heavy lifting. Plus, there’s a cardiovascular benefit to this type of movement.
